text: Neuroscientist David Badre distinguishes between two kinds of cognitive control: reactive control, which corrects errors after they occur, and proactive control, which maintains goals in working memory as a buffer against anticipated interference. The prefrontal cortex supports both, but they trade off: proactive control is metabolically expensive and tends to degrade over time, while reactive control is cheaper but slower. High metacognitive function involves knowing which mode the current situation demands — when to hold a goal actively in mind versus when to relax and correct on the fly. Failing to make this strategic distinction is itself a form of poor metacognition, wasting cognitive resources on vigilance when the situation does not require it.
question: What makes proactive cognitive control distinct from reactive control, according to Badre?

options.text: It is faster but less accurate than reactive control
It maintains goals in working memory as a buffer against anticipated interference
It relies on the hippocampus rather than the prefrontal cortex
It operates below the threshold of conscious awareness
